---
name: "dude-lightweight-execution"
description: "Use when Beads is unavailable, cannot be installed, intentionally not used, or the user wants to continue implementation directly from specs/<feature>/tasks.md without a Beads board."
---

# Lightweight Execution

Run defined work directly from `tasks.md` when Beads is not active.

## Purpose

Provide a first-class execution lane between Definition Only and Beads-tracked execution.

## When this lane is active

- the user cannot install or initialize Beads
- the user explicitly wants to avoid Beads for now
- a defined package exists and execution should continue from `specs/<feature>/tasks.md`
- Beads has not imported the feature yet

Once `@dude track` imports the feature into Beads, stop using this skill and switch to `dude-beads-workflow`.

## Source of truth

- `specs/<feature>/tasks.md` is the live markdown execution board.
- A Dude-generated board region may appear near the top of `tasks.md`, fenced by Dude-owned HTML comments `<!-- dude:board:start -->` and `<!-- dude:board:end -->`.
- The first line inside the fence must be the literal notice `<!-- generated by @dude — do not hand-edit; regenerated on every status/define refresh -->`.
- The fenced region is regenerated as a **complete replacement** on every refresh; never patch it in place. Any human edits inside the fence are discarded on the next regeneration.
- That derived board region may include `## Ready Now`, `## In Progress`, `## Blocked`, and `## Done` sections. It is a computed view, not the canonical task state.
- Canonical state lives in the phased task units **below** the fenced board region: one task header line plus any indented metadata lines such as `deps:` or `blocked-by:`.
- The first line of the canonical task list (immediately after the closing board fence) should carry the notice `<!-- canonical task units — edit task descriptions here, but let @dude mutate state glyphs -->` so editors see the boundary.
- The companion `brainstorm/<slug>.md` carries the append-only `## Coordinator Log` where Dude records every coordinator-owned mutation: state changes, board regenerations, reconciliations, and accepted manual completions.
- `spec.md`, `plan.md`, and supporting artifacts remain reference context.
- Supporting checklist files remain advisory reference unless the project explicitly says otherwise.
- Do not treat supporting checklist files as a second live execution board.
- `brainstorm/<slug>.md` remains the place to change feature intent; rerun `@dude define <feature>` if definition changes.
- Do not create a second task file, hidden execution ledger, or parallel status document.

## Task state

- `- [ ]` means not started.
- `- [~]` means in progress.
- `- [!]` means blocked.
- `- [x]` means done in Lightweight Execution.
- Lightweight task lines use durable task IDs such as `T001@a1b2c3d4`.
- Canonical task headers use this shape:

```markdown
- [ ] T001@a1b2c3d4 [P] [US1|Shared] Description with file paths
   deps: T000@e4f5g6h7, T002@91ac4e2f
   blocked-by: spec-gap: contract still needs a retry policy
```

- `deps:` is optional. It adds explicit blockers by durable task key on top of the normal phase-order rules.
- `blocked-by:` is optional, but should be present when a task is marked `[!]` and the blocker can be summarized clearly.
- Keep the task ID, labels, description, and metadata stable where the task still means the same work.
- Only the coordinator mutates task-state glyphs and task metadata in `tasks.md`; specialists report progress, blockers, and completion back to Dude.
- A bounded task may cover closely related code, tests, and docs when one fresh verification command proves the full slice; do not split work just to separate artifact types.
- If a task is blocked, mark it `[!]`, record a `blocked-by:` note when practical, and route the blocker with `@dude flag ...`.
- If `define` is rerun, preserve existing `[x]`, `[~]`, or `[!]` state only when the durable task key still matches and the task still means the same work. If a durable key is absent, fall back to task ID, story label, and core intent. If a task is split, merged, or materially re-scoped, explain the reconciliation instead of silently mapping old state onto new work.

## Selecting work

1. Read `spec.md`, `plan.md`, and any supporting artifacts that materially apply before selecting work or routing implementation.
2. If `tasks.md` is missing, empty, or has no executable task lines yet, stop and tell the user to run `@dude define <feature>` first.
3. If no non-done task units remain, report Lightweight Execution complete and recommend closing out the feature or using `@dude track` if the user now wants Beads.
4. Resume any clearly in-progress `[~]` task from current conversation context before choosing new work.
5. Otherwise read `tasks.md` and pick one next eligible ready task by default, using this order:
   - prefer the generated `## Ready Now` section when it is present and consistent with the canonical task units
   - treat `[!]` tasks as unavailable until their blocker is resolved
   - if a task has `deps:`, require those durable-key dependencies first
   - otherwise fall back to the phase order defined in `dude-feature-definition` (Setup -> Foundational -> User Story -> Polish)
   - within a phase, non-`[P]` tasks follow order and `[P]` tasks may run together when safe under `dude-parallel-dispatch`
6. Route to the narrowest credible specialist.

## Completion

- Specialists report implementation results back to the coordinator; they do not mutate task glyphs or metadata themselves.
- When a task is actively claimed or resumed in Lightweight Execution, only the coordinator may move it from `[ ]` to `[~]`.
- When a task blocks on a routed issue, only the coordinator may move it from `[ ]` or `[~]` to `[!]` and add or refresh `blocked-by:`.
- After the normal delivery pipeline produces fresh evidence, only the coordinator marks the task header `[x]`.
- When a blocker resolves without completion, the coordinator returns the task from `[!]` to `[ ]` or `[~]` based on whether work is immediately resuming.
- Keep `tasks.md` and implementation state aligned; do not report completion or blockage without updating the canonical task unit.
- If new work is discovered, either route it back through the normal definition loop if it changes scope, or ask the user whether to re-define the package before inventing ad hoc task lines.

### Detecting human-applied `[x]` without evidence

Users will sometimes self-check `[x]` in `tasks.md` by reflex. On every `status`, `define`, or implementation routing pass, compare current task glyphs against the coordinator's last known state for the file:

- If a task header has flipped to `[x]` since the last coordinator-owned write and there is no fresh verification evidence in the conversation or `## Coordinator Log`, **downgrade it to `[~]`** and post a one-line note in the response that leads with the escape hatch, not the rule. Use this exact shape:

  `T0NN@xxxxxxxx reverted to [~]. If you verified it manually, reply 'accept T0NN' and I'll record it. Otherwise the lightweight close protocol will mark it done after verification.`

- If multiple tasks were checked at once, group them into one note: `T003, T004 reverted to [~]. Reply 'accept T003, T004' to record manual verification, or let the close protocol run.`
- If the user replies `accept T0NN` (or `accept T0NN: <evidence>`), accept the `[x]`, append a `accepted manual completion of T0NN — <evidence-or-attestation>` line to `## Coordinator Log`, and do not downgrade again.
- If the user explicitly says "I verified this manually" alongside the original check, treat that as an inline accept and skip the downgrade.
- This guard does not apply to `[ ] -> [~]` or `[ ] -> [!]` user edits; those are treated as advisory hints that the coordinator should reconcile on the next routing pass, not as silent state injection.

## Lightweight close protocol

When a Lightweight Execution task reaches a completion claim:

1. collect the implementation result from the owning specialist
2. route verification to `@dude-tester` when relevant or required
3. route independent readiness judgment to `@dude-reviewer` when that role exists or the user asked for it
4. load `dude-verification-before-completion`
5. only then mark the task `[x]`, refresh or describe the derived board view, and report the next ready task or remaining blocker
6. after the canonical task glyph is updated and the board region (if present) is regenerated, run the `dude-lint` skill (`pwsh .github/skills/dude-lint/lint.ps1` or `bash .github/skills/dude-lint/lint.sh`) to confirm `tasks.md` still has balanced board fences, valid glyphs, and durable IDs. Fix any `[FAIL]` before reporting completion.

If `@dude-tester` or `@dude-reviewer` is absent, adapt the pipeline, but do not skip the fresh-evidence requirement.

## Status

`@dude status` stays read-only in this lane. It should report:

- current lane: Lightweight Execution
- live artifact: `specs/<feature>/tasks.md`
- total / not started / in progress / blocked / done task counts
- the ready-now task or parallel-safe ready set
- any in-progress task currently being resumed
- active blocker summaries when `[!]` tasks exist
- that the active feature is in Lightweight Execution and has not yet been imported into Beads
- Lightweight Execution complete when no task headers remain outside `[x]`

`@dude status` may recompute the board state in its response when the generated board region is missing or stale, but it must not rewrite `tasks.md` while answering.

## Flagging

`@dude flag ...` works the same as in tracked execution, but without creating Beads issues.

When a task blocks in this lane, the coordinator should mark that task `[!]`, add or refresh a `blocked-by:` note when practical, and then route the blocker.

Typed blockage prefixes are preferred for predictability, but plain-language blocker reports should still be classified by Dude when the intended type is clear.

- `spec-gap` -> `@dude-spec-lead`
- `plan-gap` -> `@dude-lead`
- `contract-mismatch` -> `@dude-spec-lead`
- `test-failure` -> `@dude-tester` or `dude-systematic-debugging`
- `external-dependency` -> escalate to the user

Keep the blocked task `[!]` until the blocker is resolved or the plan changes.

## Switching to Beads later

- If the user later enables Beads, stop using this lane and hand off through the normal `@dude track` flow.
- `dude-spec-import-to-beads` owns the import semantics, including `[ ]` / `[~]` / `[!]` mapping into Beads, skipped completed-history reporting for `[x]`, and stop conditions for `spec_path` drift, reconciliation ambiguity, or partial import failure.
- After import, Beads becomes the only live execution board and source of truth. `tasks.md` may still receive one-way Beads-derived mirror updates for portability, but it is not authoritative while tracked execution is active.

## Switching Back From Beads

If the user needs to continue without Beads after tracked execution has started, first prefer an explicit `@dude sync Beads to tasks.md` while Beads is still available. That sync makes `tasks.md` current as a Beads-derived snapshot before Lightweight Execution resumes.

If Beads is already unavailable, do not assume stale `tasks.md` state is complete. Report that the file is only as current as the last successful Beads-to-markdown mirror, then ask the user whether to continue from that snapshot or wait until Beads is available for a full sync. Once the user chooses to resume from the snapshot, `tasks.md` becomes the live markdown execution board again and normal Lightweight Execution rules apply.

## Avoid

- do not use this lane after Beads import
- do not create a second markdown board
- do not hand-edit the generated board region instead of the canonical task units
- do not invent task states beyond `[ ]`, `[~]`, `[!]`, and `[x]`
- do not mark tasks `[x]` before fresh verification evidence exists
- do not let specialists mark task glyphs directly
- do not silently rewrite task state or task metadata during re-define without reconciliation
